title: Your First Redstone Build: A 2-Input AND Gate
description: Build a working AND gate from redstone dust, levers, and a redstone torch. Explore spatial coordinates, block placement rules, and Boolean logic through hands-on construction.

steps.instruction: Place a 5x3 flat stone platform starting at coordinates 0,64,0. Use F3 to verify your position. The platform should extend from X=0 to X=4, Z=0 to Z=2, all at Y=64.
steps.expectedOutcome: You have a flat 5x3 stone surface visible in the F3 debug screen at Y=64. You understand that all redstone circuitry must be placed on a solid block surface.
steps.hint: F3 shows XYZ as block coordinates. The block you are standing on is one below your feet position.

steps.instruction: Place two levers at X=0,Z=0 and X=0,Z=2 (left and right inputs). These are your A and B inputs. Place redstone dust in a line from each lever toward the center at X=2.
steps.expectedOutcome: Two separate redstone lines run from the levers toward the center. Activating either lever alone powers only its own line -- the lines do not yet interact.
steps.hint: Redstone dust carries signal up to 15 blocks before needing a repeater. Keep your circuit small.

steps.instruction: Place a redstone torch on the side of a solid block at X=3,Z=1. The torch should be powered OFF when either input line reaches it. Place both redstone lines so they converge at this block.
steps.expectedOutcome: When BOTH levers are ON, the block at X=3,Z=1 receives power from both sides, which extinguishes the torch. When either lever is OFF, the torch stays lit.
steps.hint: A redstone torch on a block inverts the block's power state. An active block (powered from any side) turns off its attached torch.

steps.instruction: Extend a redstone wire from the torch output to a lamp at X=4,Z=1. Test all four input combinations (OFF/OFF, ON/OFF, OFF/ON, ON/ON) and record whether the lamp lights.
steps.expectedOutcome: Lamp lights only when BOTH levers are ON -- this is AND gate behavior. Truth table: 0,0=0 | 1,0=0 | 0,1=0 | 1,1=1.
steps.hint: Draw the truth table before testing. If results do not match, check that both input lines actually reach the torch block.

steps.instruction: Add a 2-tick repeater (right-click twice) between each lever and the central block. Observe whether the gate still works. Measure the signal delay with a stopwatch.
steps.expectedOutcome: The gate still functions correctly but now has a 0.2-second delay (2 repeater ticks x 0.1s each). You have built a timed AND gate with signal amplification for longer circuits.
steps.hint: Repeaters both amplify (reset signal to strength 15) and delay (1-4 ticks = 0.1-0.4 seconds)

steps.instruction: Build a second AND gate adjacent to the first. Connect both AND gate outputs into a single OR gate (two redstone lines merging at one point). You now have an A AND B OR C AND D circuit.
steps.expectedOutcome: A 4-input programmable logic structure. Combining AND and OR gates is the foundation of all combinational logic -- the same principles used in electronic circuits and CPU arithmetic units.
steps.hint: OR is just two lines merging -- redstone dust naturally ORs two inputs by combining their signals
